transcript: [music] >> Exercise number one. Incline barbell press. Lie on an incline bench set at 30° to 45° and grip the barbell slightly wider than shoulder width. Unrack the bar and hold it above your upper chest with your arms fully extended. Lower the bar slowly to your upper chest while keeping your elbows at about a 45° angle. Pause briefly, then press the bar upward until your arms are fully extended without locking your elbows. Keep your feet flat on the floor, your back firmly against the bench, and avoid bouncing the bar off your chest. This compound exercise primarily targets the upper chest while also engaging the front deltoids and triceps, making it excellent for building upper chest size and pressing strength. Exercise number two. Seated machine fly. Adjust the machine so the handles are level with your chest. Sit upright with your back against the pad and grasp the handles with a slight bend in your elbows. Bring the handles together in front of your chest by squeezing your pectoral muscles. Hold the contraction for a second, then slowly return to the starting position until you feel a comfortable stretch across your chest. Avoid locking your elbows or using momentum. Maintain controlled movement throughout each repetition. The seated machine fly isolates the chest muscles, helping improve muscle definition, shape, and overall chest development while minimizing shoulder involvement. Exercise number three. High to low cable fly. Set both cable pulleys to the highest position and attach single handles. Stand in the center with one foot slightly forward and hold the handles with a slight bend in your elbows. Pull the cables downward and inward in a wide arc until your hands meet near your lower abdomen. Squeeze your chest at the bottom, then slowly return to the starting position while maintaining tension. Keep your chest up and avoid excessive shoulder movement. This exercise emphasizes the lower chest fibers while also improving chest definition, muscle control, and overall upper body symmetry. Exercise number four. Machine triceps dips. Sit comfortably on the machine and grip the handles with your elbows bent. Keep your back against the pad and your elbows close to your sides. Push the handles downward by extending your elbows until your arms are nearly straight. Squeeze your triceps at the bottom of the movement, then slowly return to the starting position under control. Avoid using momentum or shrugging your shoulders. Maintain a smooth and controlled tempo throughout the exercise. Seated machine triceps dips effectively isolate [bell] all three heads of the triceps, helping build stronger, thicker, and more defined upper arms. Exercise number five. Dumbbell overhead extension. Sit or stand upright while holding a dumbbell with both hands above your head. Keep your elbows close to your ears and your upper arms stationary. Lower the dumbbell slowly behind your head by bending your elbows until you feel a stretch in your triceps. Pause briefly, then extend your elbows to raise the dumbbell back to the starting position. Keep your core engaged and avoid arching your lower back. Perform the movement slowly for for muscle activation. This exercise mainly targets the long head of the triceps, helping increase arm size, strength, and overall muscle definition. Exercise number six. Cable rope push down. Attach a rope handle to a high cable pulley and stand facing the machine. Hold the rope with a neutral grip and keep your elbows tucked close to your sides. Begin with the rope at chest level, then push it downward by extending your elbows until your arms are fully straight. At the bottom, separate the rope ends slightly and squeeze your triceps. Slowly return to the starting position while maintaining constant tension. Keep your upper arm stationary and avoid swinging your body. Cable rope push downs [bell] effectively target all three heads of the triceps, improving arm strength, muscle size, and definition.

[music] >> Exercise number one. Barbell skull crushers. Lie flat on a bench holding a barbell with a shoulder width grip. Extend your arms straight above your chest. Keeping your upper arm stationary, bend your elbows to lower the bar towards your forehead or just behind your head. Pause briefly when you feel a deep stretch in your triceps. Then extend your elbows to return the bar to the starting position. Perform the movement slowly and avoid flaring your elbows outward. Keep your wrists straight throughout the exercise. Barbell skull crushers primarily target all three heads of the triceps, helping increase arm size, strength, and overall pressing power. Exercise number two. Barbell curl. Stand upright holding a barbell with an underhand grip slightly wider than shoulder width. Keep your elbows close to your torso and your chest lifted. Curl the bar upward by bending your elbows while keeping your upper arm stationary. Raise the bar until your biceps are fully contracted, then pause briefly and squeeze the muscles. Lower the bar slowly to the starting position under control. Avoid swinging your body or using momentum. Barbell curls mainly [bell] target the biceps brachii while also engaging the brachialis and forearm muscles for thicker, stronger arms. Exercise number three. Seated dumbbell overhead extension. Sit upright on a bench with back support and hold one dumbbell with both hands above your head. Keep your elbows close to your ears and your upper arm stationary. Lower the dumbbell slowly behind your head by bending your elbows until you feel a stretch in your triceps. Pause briefly, then extend your elbows to raise the dumbbell back overhead. Avoid arching your lower back or flaring your elbows. This exercise emphasizes the long head of the triceps, helping build thicker, stronger, and more defined upper arms. Exercise number four, alternating dumbbell curl. Stand with a dumbbell in each hand, arms fully extended and palms facing inward. Curl one dumbbell toward your shoulder while rotating your wrist so your palm faces upward. Squeeze your biceps at the top, then lower the weight slowly to the starting position. Repeat the movement with the opposite arm. Keep your elbows close to your body and avoid swinging your torso. Perform each repetition with control to maximize muscle activation. Alternating dumbbell curls isolate each arm individually, improving bicep size, strength, symmetry, and peak development. Exercise number five, V-bar cable triceps pushdown. Attach a V-bar handle to a high cable pulley and stand facing the machine. Grip the handle firmly and keep your elbows tucked close to your sides. Start with the handle near your upper chest. Push the V-bar downward by extending your elbows until your arms are fully straight. Squeeze your triceps at the bottom of the movement, then slowly return to the starting position while maintaining tension. Avoid moving your upper arms or using momentum. This exercise effectively targets all three heads of the triceps, promoting greater arm size, strength, and muscle definition. Exercise number six, Seated dumbbell hammer curl. Sit upright on a bench holding a dumbbell in each hand with your palms facing each other. Keep your elbows close to your sides and curl one dumbbell

[music] >> Exercise number one. Close grip bench press. Lie flat on a bench and grip the barbell slightly narrower than shoulder width. Unrack the bar and hold it above your chest with your arms extended. Lower the bar slowly toward your lower chest while keeping your elbows close to your body. Pause briefly when the bar touches your chest, then press it back up until your arms are fully extended. Keep your wrists straight and avoid flaring your elbows outward. This compound exercise primarily targets the triceps while also engaging the chest and front deltoids, making it excellent for building arm strength and pressing power. Exercise number two. Cable rope push down. Attach a rope handle to a high cable pulley and stand facing the machine. Grip the rope with both hands and keep your elbows tucked close to your sides. Start with the rope near your upper chest. Push the rope downward by extending your elbows until your arms are fully straight. At the bottom, separate the rope ends slightly and squeeze your triceps. Slowly return to the starting position while maintaining tension. Avoid using momentum or moving your upper arms. Cable rope push downs effectively isolate all three heads of the triceps and help increase arm size, strength, and muscle definition. Exercise number three. Seated cable overhead extension. Sit on a bench facing away from a low cable pulley and attach a rope handle. Hold the rope behind your head with both hands and keep your elbows pointing upward. Extend your arms overhead until they are fully straight while keeping your upper arm stationary. Pause briefly at the top and squeeze your triceps. Lower the rope slowly behind your head to feel a stretch in the triceps. Maintain an upright posture throughout the movement. >> [bell] >> This exercise emphasizes the long head of the triceps, helping to build thicker and more developed upper arms. Exercise number four. Dumbbell skull crushers. Lie flat on a bench with a dumbbell in each hand and your arms extended above your chest. Keep your upper arm stationary and bend your elbows to lower the dumbbells toward the sides of your head. Pause briefly when you feel a stretch in your triceps. Extend your elbows to return the dumbbells to the starting position. Perform the movement slowly and under control without letting your elbows flare excessively. Dumbbell skull crushers provide an excellent stretch and contraction for the triceps, making them highly effective for increasing arm size and strength. Exercise number five. Machine triceps dips. Sit on a triceps dip machine and place your hands on the handles. Adjust the seat so your elbows are bent comfortably at the starting position. Press the handles downward by extending your elbows until your arms are fully straight. Squeeze your triceps at the bottom of the movement, then slowly return to the starting position. Keep your shoulders stable and avoid using momentum. Machine triceps dips offer a controlled movement pattern that effectively isolates the triceps while reducing stress on the shoulders and improving overall arm strength. Exercise number six. Single arm dumbbell kickback. Hold a dumbbell in one hand and place your opposite hand on a bench for support. Bend forward at the hips while keeping your back flat. Position your upper arm close to your torso with your elbow bent at 90°. Extend your arm backward until it is fully straight and parallel to the floor. Squeeze your triceps at the top of the movement, then slowly return to the starting position. Keep your upper arm stationary throughout the exercise. >> [bell] >> Single arm dumbbell kickbacks are excellent for isolating the triceps and improving muscle definition and contraction.

[music] >> Exercise number one. V-bar cable triceps push down. Stand facing a cable machine with a V-bar attachment connected to the high pulley. Grip the V-bar with both hands and keep your elbows tucked close to your sides. Start with your forearms bent and the bar near your upper chest. Push the bar downward by extending your elbows until your arms are fully straight. Squeeze your triceps at the bottom of the movement, then slowly return to the starting position. Avoid swinging your body or moving your elbows forward. This exercise effectively targets all three heads of the triceps, helping to increase arm size and strength. Exercise number two. Easy bar curl. Stand upright holding an easy bar with an underhand grip. Keep your elbows close to your torso and your chest up. Curl the bar upward by bending your elbows while keeping your upper arm stationary. Raise the bar until your biceps are fully contracted. Pause briefly at the top and squeeze your biceps, then lower the bar slowly to the starting position. Avoid using momentum or swinging your body. Easy bar curls primarily target the biceps brachii and also engage the brachialis and forearm muscles. Exercise number three. Weighted triceps dips parallel bar. Position yourself between parallel bars and attach a weight belt if additional resistance is needed. Support your body with straight arms and crossed legs. Lower yourself by bending your elbows until your upper arms are approximately parallel to the floor. Keep your torso upright to emphasize the triceps. Push through your palms to extend your elbows and return to the starting position. Maintain control throughout the movement. Weighted triceps dips are an excellent compound exercise for building triceps mass, strength, and overall upper body power. Exercise number four. Seated dumbbell curl. Sit upright on a bench with a dumbbell in each hand and your arms fully extended at your sides. Keep your elbows close to your body and curl the dumbbells upward by bending your elbows. Rotate your palms upward as you lift the weights. Squeeze your biceps at the top of the movement, then lower the dumbbells slowly back to the starting position. Avoid swinging your arms or leaning backward. This exercise isolates the biceps and helps improve arm size, peak development, and muscle definition. Exercise number five. Inclined dumbbell skull crushers. Lie on an incline bench while holding a pair of dumbbells above your chest with your arms fully extended. Keep your upper arm stationary and bend your elbows to lower the dumbbells toward the sides of your head. Pause briefly when you feel a stretch in your triceps. Extend your elbows to raise the dumbbells back to the starting position. Perform the movement with control and avoid flaring your elbows excessively. This exercise places a deep stretch on the triceps, promoting muscle growth and strength. Exercise number six. Seated dumbbell preacher curl. Sit at a preacher bench with the back of your upper arm resting firmly on the preacher pad. Hold a dumbbell with one hand and start with your arm nearly straight. Curl the dumbbell upward toward your shoulder while keeping your upper arm fixed on the pad. Squeeze your biceps at the top. Then lower the weight slowly until your arm is fully extended. Avoid lifting your elbow off the pad. This exercise isolates the biceps and minimizes momentum helping build arm size and peak contraction. Exercise number seven. Cable rope push down. Attach a rope handle to a high cable pulley and stand facing the machine. Grip the rope with a neutral grip and keep your elbows tucked close to your sides. Push the rope downward until your arms are fully extended spreading the rope ends apart at the bottom. Squeeze your triceps then slowly return to the starting position. Keep your upper arm stationary throughout the exercise. Cable rope push downs effectively target the triceps especially the lateral and long heads while providing constant muscle tension. Exercise number eight. Cable curl. Stand facing a low cable pulley with a straight bar or curl attachment. Grip the handle with an underhand grip and keep your elbows close to your sides. Curl the handle upward towards your shoulders by bending your elbows. Squeeze your biceps at the top of the movement then slowly lower the handle back to the starting position. Maintain tension on the cable throughout the exercise and avoid using momentum. Cable curls provide constant resistance making them highly effective for building biceps size strength and muscle definition.

[music] >> Exercise number one. Dumbbell shrugs. Stand upright with a dumbbell in each hand at your sides. Keep your arms straight and your chest up. Slowly raise your shoulders towards your ears as high as possible without bending your elbows. Pause briefly at the top and squeeze your trapezius muscles. Lower your shoulders back down in a controlled manner. Avoid rolling your shoulders during the movement. Dumbbell shrugs primarily target the [bell] upper trapezius muscles, helping to build thicker and stronger traps while improving shoulder stability and posture. Exercise number two. Easy bar upright row. Stand with your feet shoulder width apart and hold an easy bar with a narrow overhead grip. Keep the bar close to your body and lift it straight upward by driving your elbows toward the ceiling. Raise the bar until it reaches upper chest level. Pause briefly, then lower the bar slowly to the starting position. Keep your torso upright throughout the exercise. Easy bar upright rows mainly work the lateral deltoids and trapezius muscles, helping to increase shoulder width and upper back development. Exercise number three. Dumbbell lateral raise. Stand or sit upright while holding a dumbbell in each hand at your sides. Keep a slight bend in your elbows and your palms facing inward. Raise the dumbbells out to the sides until they reach shoulder height. Pause briefly at the top while squeezing your side deltoids. Lower the weight slowly and under control to the starting position. Avoid swinging your body or using momentum. Dumbbell lateral raises are one of the best isolation exercises for building wider shoulders and enhancing shoulder definition. Exercise number four. Seated Smith machine shoulder press. Sit on a bench positioned under a Smith machine and grip the bar slightly wider than shoulder width. Start with the bar at shoulder level. Press the bar upward until your arms are fully extended overhead. Pause briefly at the top. Then lower the bar slowly back to shoulder height. Keep your back firmly against the bench and your core engaged throughout the movement. This exercise primarily targets the front [bell] and side deltoids, while also engaging the triceps, making it excellent for overall shoulder size and strength. Exercise number five. Seated dumbbell shoulder press. Sit on a bench with back support and hold a dumbbell in each hand at shoulder height. Keep your palms facing forward and your feet flat on the floor. Press the dumbbells overhead until your arms are nearly straight. Pause briefly, then lower the weight slowly back to the starting position. Maintain a controlled motion and avoid arching your lower back. This compound exercise targets the anterior and lateral deltoids, while also strengthening the triceps and upper chest, helping build bigger and stronger shoulders. Exercise number six. Cable rope face pull. Attach a rope handle to a high cable pulley and stand facing the machine. Grab the rope with both hands and step back to create tension. Pull the rope toward your face while keeping your elbows high and flaring outward. Squeeze your rear deltoids and upper back muscles at the end of the movement. Slowly return to the starting position under control. Cable rope face pulls target the rear deltoids, traps, and rotator cuff muscles improving shoulder health, posture, and overall upper body balance.

[music] >> Exercise number one. Flat bench barbell press. Lie flat on a bench with your feet planted firmly on the floor. Grip the barbell slightly wider than shoulder width and lift it off the rack. Lower the bar under control to the middle of your chest while keeping your shoulders retracted and elbows slightly tucked. Press the bar upward until your arms are fully extended without locking the elbows excessively. Maintain a stable body position throughout the movement. This exercise primarily targets the chest while also engaging the shoulders and triceps. Exercise number two. Incline dumbbell press. Adjust a bench to a 30 45° incline and sit back with a dumbbell in each hand. Hold the dumbbells at chest level with palms facing forward. Press the weights upward and slightly inward until your arms are nearly straight. Pause briefly at the top then lower the dumbbells slowly back to the starting position. Keep your shoulders down and back throughout the movement. This exercise emphasizes the upper chest while also working the front deltoids and triceps. Exercise number three. Seated barbell shoulder press. Sit upright on a bench with back support and hold the barbell at shoulder height using an overhand grip. Keep your core tight and feet flat on the floor. Press the bar straight overhead until your arms are fully extended. Avoid arching your lower back as you lift. Lower the bar slowly back to shoulder level and repeat. Focus on controlled movement and proper posture. This exercise primarily develops the front and side deltoids while also strengthening the triceps and upper chest. Exercise number four. Seated dumbbell lateral raise. Sit upright on a bench holding a dumbbell in each hand at your sides. Keep a slight bend in your elbows and your palms facing inward. Raise the dumbbells outward until they reach shoulder height leading with your elbows rather than your hands. Pause briefly at the top then lower the weight slowly and under control. >> [bell] >> Avoid swinging your body or using momentum. This exercise effectively isolates the lateral deltoids helping to build shoulder width and improve overall shoulder definition. Exercise number five. High to low cable fly. Set the cable pulleys at a high position and grasp the handles with both hands. Step forward into a stable stance and keep a slight bend in your elbows. Pull the handles downward and inward in a wide arc until your hands meet in front of your lower chest or upper abdomen. Squeeze your chest muscles at the bottom of the movement then slowly return to the starting position. Keep your torso still throughout the exercise. This movement targets the lower portion of the chest and improves chest definition. Exercise number six. Easy bar skull crushers. Lie flat on a bench while holding an easy bar with a shoulder width grip. Extend your arms straight above your chest. Keeping your upper arm stationary bend your elbows to lower the bar towards your forehead or just behind your head. Pause briefly when your elbows are fully bent then extend your arms to return to the starting position. Perform the movement slowly and with control. This isolation exercise focuses on the triceps and helps increase arm size and strength. Exercise number seven. Cable triceps push down. Stand facing a cable machine and grasp the attachment with both hands. Keep your elbows close to your sides and your upper arms stationary throughout the movement. Push the handle downward by extending your elbows until your arms are fully straight. Squeeze your triceps at the bottom, then slowly allow the handle to return to the starting position. Avoid using momentum or moving your shoulders. This exercise effectively [bell] isolates all three heads of the triceps and is excellent for building arm strength and muscle definition.

[music] >> Exercise number one. Incline barbell press. Lie on an incline bench set at about 30° to 45° and grip the barbell slightly wider than shoulder width. Unrack the bar and lower it slowly to the upper chest while keeping your elbows under control. Press the bar upward until your arms are nearly straight, then repeat. This exercise primarily targets the upper chest, front shoulders, and triceps, helping to build strength and muscle mass in the upper pectoral region. Exercise number two. Seated machine fly. Sit on the machine with your back firmly against the pad and grasp the handles with a slight bend in your elbows. Bring the handles together in front of your chest by squeezing your pectoral muscles. Pause briefly at the peak contraction, then slowly return to the starting position. This [bell] isolation exercise effectively targets the chest muscles, enhancing muscle definition and improving chest shape. Exercise number three. Machine chest press. Sit or lie on the chest press machine with your feet firmly planted and hands gripping the handles. Push the handles forward until your arms are almost fully extended without locking the elbows. Slowly return to the starting position while maintaining control of the weight. This compound movement primarily works the chest, triceps, and front shoulders, making it excellent for building upper body strength and muscle size. Exercise number four. High to low cable fly. Stand between two high cable pulleys and grasp the handles with your palms facing downward. Step forward slightly and pull the handles downward and inward in a wide arc until they meet in front of your lower chest. Squeeze your chest muscles at the bottom, then slowly return to the starting position. This exercise emphasizes the lower chest while improving muscle definition and chest development. Exercise number five. Dumbbell overhead extension. Hold a dumbbell with both hands and raise it overhead while keeping your arms extended. Lower the weight slowly behind your head by bending your elbows, keeping your upper arm stationary. Extend your elbows to return the dumbbell to the starting position. This exercise primarily targets the long head of the triceps, helping to improve arm size, strength, and overall muscle definition. Exercise number six. Cable rope pushdown. Attach a rope to a high cable pulley and stand upright while gripping the rope with both hands. Keep your elbows close to your sides and push the rope downward until your arms are fully extended. At the bottom, separate the rope ends slightly and squeeze your triceps. Slowly return to the starting position. This [bell] exercise effectively isolates the triceps, enhancing arm strength, muscle growth, and definition.

[music] >> Exercise number one. Seated barbell curl. Sit upright on a bench with your feet flat on the floor. Hold a barbell with an underhand grip. Hand shoulder width apart. And let your arms hang straight down. Keeping your elbows close to your torso. Curl the barbell upward by contracting your biceps. Lift until the bar reaches shoulder level. Then slowly lower it back to the starting position. Avoid swinging your body or using momentum. Maintain a controlled movement throughout the exercise to maximize biceps activation and muscle growth. Exercise number two. Barbell reverse grip curl. Stand with your feet shoulder width apart. And hold a barbell using an overhand grip. Keep your elbows close to your sides and your chest lifted. Curl the bar upward while keeping your wrists straight. And your upper arm stationary. Raise the bar until your forearms are nearly vertical. Then slowly lower it back down. Focus on controlled movement without swinging your body. >> [bell] >> This exercise primarily targets the brachioradialis and forearm muscles. While also engaging the biceps. Exercise number three. Cable curl. Stand facing a low cable pulley. And attach a straight bar or easy bar handle. Hold the attachment with an underhand grip. And keep your elbows tucked to your sides. Curl the handle upward by squeezing your biceps. While keeping your upper arm still. Lift until your hands reach shoulder height. Then slowly return to the starting position. Maintain constant tension throughout the movement. And avoid leaning backward. >> [bell] >> Cable curls provide continuous resistance, helping to improve muscle contraction and overall biceps development. Exercise number four. Single arm dumbbell preacher curl. Sit at a preacher bench and place one upper arm firmly against the preacher pad. Hold a dumbbell with an underhand grip and extend your arm fully. Curl the dumbbell upward while keeping your upper arm fixed against the pad. Squeeze your biceps at the top of the movement, then slowly lower the weight back to the starting position. Perform all repetitions on one arm before switching sides. This exercise isolates the biceps and minimizes momentum for better muscle engagement. Exercise number five. Cross body dumbbell hammer curl. Stand tall with a dumbbell in each hand, palms facing your body. Curl one dumbbell across your torso toward the opposite shoulder while maintaining a neutral grip. Keep your elbow close to your side and avoid swinging your body. Lower the dumbbell slowly and repeat with the opposite arm. Continue alternating arms for the desired number of repetitions. This exercise targets the brachialis, brachioradialis, and biceps, helping to build thicker and stronger upper arms and forearms. Exercise number six. Barbell 21 curl. Hold a barbell with an underhand grip and stand with your feet shoulder width apart. Perform seven partial reps from the bottom position to halfway up, then seven partial reps from halfway to the top. Finish with seven full range repetitions from bottom to top. Keep your elbows close to your sides and maintain strict form throughout the set. Move in a controlled manner without using momentum. Barbell 21s create intense muscle fatigue and are highly effective for increasing bicep size, endurance, and muscle pump.

[music] >> Exercise number one. Incline bench dumbbell press. Lie on an incline bench set at about 30 to 45° with a dumbbell in each hand. Hold the dumbbells at chest level with palms facing forward. Press the weights upward until your arms are fully extended above your upper chest. Pause briefly at the top, then slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position. Keep your feet flat on the floor, chest lifted, and shoulder blades retracted throughout the movement. This exercise primarily targets the upper chest, front deltoids, and triceps. Exercise number two. Incline dumbbell fly. Lie on an incline bench with a dumbbell in each hand held above your chest, palms facing each other. Keep a slight bend in your elbows, and slowly lower the dumbbells out to the sides in a wide arc until you feel a stretch in your chest. Squeeze your chest muscles to bring the dumbbells back together above your chest. Avoid bending your elbows excessively or lowering the weights too far. This exercise emphasizes the upper chest and improves chest muscle stretch and contraction. Exercise number three. Lever decline chest press. Sit on the machine and adjust the seat so the handles align with your lower chest. Grasp the handles firmly and keep your back against the pad. Push the handles forward until your arms are nearly straight without locking the elbows. Pause briefly, then slowly return to the starting position under control. Keep your chest up and shoulders back throughout the movement. This exercise mainly targets the lower chest, front deltoids, and triceps while providing a stable pressing path. Exercise number four. Machine chest fly. Sit on the machine with your back firmly against the pad and grasp the handles. Start with your arms extended out to the sides and elbows slightly bent. Bring the handles together in front of your chest by squeezing your pectoral muscles. Pause briefly when the handles meet, then slowly return to the starting position. Maintain controlled movement and avoid using momentum. Machine chest fly effectively isolates the chest muscles, especially the inner and middle portions of the pectoralis major. Exercise number five. Bodyweight dips. Position yourself on parallel bars with your arms fully extended and body supported. Lean slightly forward and slowly lower your body by bending your elbows until your upper arms are about parallel to the floor. Push through your palms to return to the starting position. Keep your chest lifted and core engaged throughout the exercise. Avoid excessive swinging. Bodyweight dips primarily target the lower chest, triceps, and front deltoids, making them one of the best bodyweight exercises for upper body strength. Exercise number six. Push-ups. Start in a plank position with your hands slightly wider than shoulder-width apart and your body in a straight line. Lower your chest toward the floor by bending your elbows while keeping your core tight. Descend until your chest is just above the ground, then push through your palms to return to the starting position. Keep your head neutral and avoid sagging your hips. Push-ups are an excellent compound exercise that develops the chest, shoulders, triceps, and core muscles simultaneously.

[music] >> Exercise number one. Smith machine incline press. Set an incline bench at about 30° to 45° under the Smith machine bar. Lie back with your feet flat on the floor and grip the bar slightly wider than shoulder width. Un-rack the bar and lower it slowly to your upper chest while keeping your elbows at a comfortable angle. Pause briefly, then press the bar upward until your arms are almost fully extended without locking the elbows. Keep your chest lifted, shoulders retracted, and core engaged throughout the movement. Perform the exercise in a controlled manner to effectively target the upper chest, front shoulders, and triceps. Exercise number two. Dumbbell pullover. Lie flat on a bench with your upper back supported and feet firmly on the floor. Hold the dumbbell with both hands, gripping the inside of the weight plate, and extend it directly above your chest. Keeping a slight bend in your elbows, slowly lower the dumbbell backward in an arc until you feel a stretch in your chest and lats. Pause briefly at the bottom, then pull the dumbbell back to the starting position using your chest and back muscles. Maintain a tight core and avoid excessive arching of the lower back throughout the movement. Exercise number three. High to low cable fly. Set both cable pulleys to the highest position and attach single handles. Stand between the cables with one foot slightly forward for balance. Grasp the handles with a slight bend in your elbows and step forward until you feel tension in your chest. Keeping your chest up and core engaged, pull the handles downward and inward in a wide arc until your hands meet in front of your lower chest or waist. Squeeze your chest muscles at the bottom, then slowly return to the starting position under control. Avoid locking your elbows or using momentum throughout the movement. Exercise number four. Machine fly. Adjust the seat so that the handles are level with your chest. Sit upright with your back firmly against the pad and place your feet flat on the floor. Grasp the handles with a slight bend in your elbows. Slowly bring the handles together in front of your chest by squeezing your chest muscles. Pause briefly at the point of maximum contraction, then return the handles to the starting position in a controlled manner until you feel a gentle stretch across your chest. >> [bell] >> Keep your shoulders down and back throughout the movement and avoid using momentum to maximize chest muscle activation. Exercise number five. Decline dumbbell fly. Lie on a decline bench with your feet securely positioned under the pads. Hold a dumbbell in each hand and extend your arms above your chest with palms facing each other. Keep a slight bend in your elbows throughout the movement. Slowly lower the dumbbells out to your sides in a wide arc until you feel a comfortable stretch in your chest. Pause briefly, then bring the dumbbells back together over your chest by squeezing your chest muscles. Maintain control throughout the exercise. Keep your shoulders stable and avoid excessive elbow bending to maximize lower chest engagement and reduce joint stress. Exercise number six. Weighted push-ups. Place a weight plate on your upper back or wear a weighted vest. Start in a standard push-up position with your hands slightly wider than shoulder width apart. Body forming a straight line from head to heels. Engage your core and keep your glutes tight throughout the movement. Slowly lower your chest toward the floor by bending your elbows while keeping them at about a 45° angle to your body. Pause briefly near the bottom, then push through your palms to return to the starting position. Maintain proper form and avoid letting your hips sag or rise during the exercise.

[music] >> Exercise number one. Easy bar preacher curl. Easy bar preacher curl is an isolation exercise that targets the biceps. Sit on a preacher bench and place the backs of your upper arms firmly against the angle pad. Hold an easy bar with an underhand grip at shoulder width. Start with your arms nearly extended keeping your chest against the pad. Curl the bar upward by contracting your biceps until it reaches shoulder level. Pause briefly at the top. Then slowly lower the bar back to the starting position under control. Avoid lifting your elbows off the pad or using momentum. Maintain a steady tempo and proper form throughout the movement. Exercise number two. Incline easy bar skull crushers. Incline easy bar skull crushers are an effective exercise for building the triceps. Set an incline bench at about 30 to 45° and lie back with your feet firmly on the floor. Hold an easy bar with a shoulder width overhand grip and extend your arms straight above your chest. Keeping your upper arm stationary bend your elbows to slowly lower the bar towards your forehead or just behind your head. Feel a stretch in the triceps at the bottom position. Then extend your elbows to press the bar back to the starting position. Avoid flaring your elbows and use controlled movement throughout the exercise. Exercise number three. Alternating dumbbell curl. Alternating dumbbell curl is a classic biceps exercise that helps build arm strength and muscle size. Stand upright with a dumbbell in each hand, arms fully extended at your sides, and palms facing inward. Keeping your elbow close to your torso, curl one dumbbell upward while rotating your palm to face your shoulder as you lift. Squeeze your biceps at the top, then slowly lower the weight back to the starting position. Repeat with the opposite arm, alternating sides for each repetition. Avoid swinging your body or using momentum. Maintain controlled movements and proper posture throughout the exercise for maximum effectiveness. Exercise number four. V-bar cable triceps pushdown. V-bar cable triceps pushdown is an effective isolation exercise for developing the triceps. Attach a V-bar handle to a high pulley cable machine and stand facing it. Grasp the handle with a neutral grip, keeping your elbows close to your sides. Start with your forearms bent and the handle near your upper chest. Push the handle downward by extending your elbows until your arms are fully straight. Squeeze your triceps briefly at the bottom position, then slowly return the handle to the starting position under control. Avoid leaning excessively or moving your elbows forward. Maintain a steady tempo and proper form throughout the exercise. Exercise number five. Incline dumbbell hammer curl. Incline dumbbell hammer curl targets the biceps and brachialis muscles. Set an incline bench to about 45 to 60° and sit back with a dumbbell in each hand, arms hanging straight down. Keep your palms facing each other throughout the movement. Without swinging your body, curl one or both dumbbells upward while keeping your elbows close to your sides. Lift until your forearms are nearly vertical, then squeeze your arm muscles at the top. Slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position under control. Maintain a neutral wrist position and avoid using momentum. Focus on smooth, controlled repetitions for maximum muscle engagement. Exercise number six. Single arm reverse grip cable push down. Single arm reverse grip cable push down is an isolation exercise that targets the triceps, especially the medial head. Attach a single handle to a high pulley and grasp it with an underhand, supinated grip. Stand upright with your elbows tucked close to your side and your forearm bent about 90°. Keeping your upper arm stationary, push the handle downward by extending your elbow until your arm is fully straight. Squeeze your triceps briefly at the bottom, then slowly return to the starting position under control. Avoid [bell] using momentum or moving your shoulder. Perform all repetitions on one arm before switching sides.

[music] >> Exercise number one. Cable seated hand grip row. Stand facing a cable machine and hold the handle with a neutral grip. Keep your feet shoulder width apart, knees slightly bent, and chest up. Extend your arms fully while maintaining a straight back. Pull the handle towards your lower ribs by driving your elbows backward and squeezing your shoulder blades together. Pause briefly at the contracted position, then slowly return to the starting position with control. Keep your core engaged and avoid using momentum or excessive body movement. Repeat for the desired number of repetitions while maintaining proper posture and a full range of motion. Exercise number two. Dumbbell incline row. Lie face down on an incline bench set at about 30° to 45°. Holding a dumbbell in each hand with your arms hanging straight down. Keep your chest against the bench, core engaged, and shoulders relaxed. Pull the dumbbells upward by driving your elbows back and squeezing your shoulder blades together. Raise the weights until your elbows are slightly above your torso. Pause briefly at the top feeling the contraction in your upper back muscles. Slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position under control. Avoid swinging the weights or lifting your chest off the bench throughout the movement. Exercise number three. Barbell bent over row. Stand with your feet shoulder width apart and hold a barbell with an overhand grip. Bend at your hips until your torso is nearly parallel to the floor keeping your back straight and core tight. Let the barbell hang at arms length beneath your shoulders. Pull the bar toward your lower chest or upper abdomen by driving your elbows backward and squeezing your shoulder blades together. Pause briefly at the top of the movement, then slowly lower the bar back to the starting position with control. Avoid using momentum or rounding your back. Repeat for the desired number of repetitions. Exercise number four. Lever lying T-bar row. Lie chest down on the padded support of a lever lying T-bar row machine and grasp the handles with a comfortable grip. Keep your chest firmly against the pad, shoulders down, and core engaged. Start with your arms fully extended. Pull the handles toward your torso by driving your elbows backward and squeezing your shoulder blades together. Pause briefly at the top to maximize back muscle contraction. Slowly lower the weight back to the starting position under control. Avoid lifting your chest off the [bell] pad or using momentum. Repeat for the desired number of repetitions while maintaining proper form throughout the exercise. Exercise number five. Inverted row slide. Set up a suspension trainer, slide board, or bar at waist height. Lie underneath it with your body straight, heels on sliders or a smooth surface, and arms fully extended. Keep your core tight and maintain a straight line from your head to your heels. Pull your chest toward the handles or bar by driving your elbows backward and squeezing your shoulder blades together. At the same time, slide your body smoothly if using sliders. Pause briefly at the top, then slowly extend your arms to return to the starting position. Avoid sagging your hips or using momentum. Repeat with controlled movement throughout the exercise. Exercise number six. Dumbbell bent over row. Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart holding a dumbbell in each hand. Bend forward at the hips until your torso is nearly parallel to the floor keeping your back straight and knees slightly bent. Let the dumbbells hang directly below your shoulders with your arms fully extended. Pull the dumbbells toward your waist by driving your elbows backward and squeezing your shoulder blades together. Pause briefly at the top of the movement then slowly lower the weights back to the starting position. Keep your core engaged and avoid swinging the weights or rounding your back. Repeat for the desired number of repetitions. Exercise number seven. Cable one-arm lat pulldown. Sit or kneel beside a cable machine with the pulley set high. Grasp the handle with one hand and fully extend your arm overhead. Keep your chest up, core engaged, and shoulders down. Pull the handle downward toward your upper chest by driving your elbow toward your side and contracting your lat muscle. Avoid leaning excessively or using momentum. Pause briefly at the bottom of the movement to maximize muscle engagement. Slowly return the handle to the starting position with control allowing your arm to fully extend. Complete the desired repetitions on one side before switching to the other arm. Exercise number eight. Dumbbell pullover. Lie flat on a bench while holding a dumbbell with both hands above your chest. Keep your feet firmly on the floor and your core engaged. Slightly bend your elbows and maintain that angle throughout the movement. Slowly lower the dumbbell backward over your head until you feel a stretch in your chest and lats. Pause briefly at the bottom, then pull the dumbbell back to the starting position by engaging your chest, lats, and shoulders. Avoid excessive arching of your lower back or bending your elbows too much. Perform the movement in a controlled manner for the desired number of repetitions. Exercise number nine. Dumbbell one-arm row. Place one knee and the same-side hand on a flat bench for support while holding a dumbbell in the opposite hand. Keep your back straight, chest up, and core engaged. Let the dumbbell hang directly below your shoulder with your arm fully extended. Pull the dumbbell toward your hip by driving your elbow upward and backward, squeezing your shoulder blade at the top of the movement. Pause briefly, then slowly lower the weight back to the starting position with control. Avoid twisting your torso or using momentum. Complete all repetitions on one side before switching to the other arm.

[music] >> Exercise number one. Chest dip. Chest dip is a powerful exercise for building the lower chest, shoulders, and triceps. Hold the dip bars firmly and lift your body with straight arms. Lean your upper body slightly forward and keep your elbows slightly outward. Slowly lower your body until your shoulders go below your elbows. Then push yourself back up to the starting position while squeezing your chest muscles. Maintain controlled movement and steady breathing throughout the exercise. Avoid swinging your body or locking your elbows too hard at the top. Exercise number two. Dumbbell incline bench press. The dumbbell incline bench press mainly targets the upper chest, shoulders, and triceps. Lie on an incline bench holding a dumbbell in each hand at chest level. Keep your feet flat on the floor and your back firmly against the bench. Press the dumbbells upward until your arms are fully extended without locking the elbows. Slowly lower the weights back to the starting position in a controlled motion. Keep your chest lifted and avoid bouncing the dumbbells. Maintain steady breathing throughout the exercise. This movement helps build upper chest strength, muscle size, and better chest definition. Exercise number three. Dumbbell bench press. The dumbbell bench press is an effective exercise for building chest strength and muscle size. Lie flat on a bench while holding a dumbbell in each hand at chest level. Keep your feet firmly on the floor and your back pressed against the bench. Press the dumbbells upward until your arms are fully extended, keeping the movement controlled. Slowly lower the dumbbells back to chest level without letting them drop too quickly. Keep your elbows slightly bent and avoid arching your back excessively. Breathe steadily throughout the exercise. This workout mainly targets the chest, shoulders, and triceps muscles. Exercise number four. Lever seated fly. Lever seated fly is an excellent exercise for targeting the chest muscles, especially the inner chest. Sit on the machine with your back firmly against the pad and hold the handles with your elbows slightly bent. Start with your arms open wide at shoulder level. Slowly bring the handles together in front of your chest while squeezing your chest muscles. Pause briefly at the center position, then slowly return to the starting position in a controlled motion. Avoid using momentum or moving too fast. Keep steady breathing throughout the exercise. This workout mainly works the chest, shoulders, and supporting arm muscles. Exercise number five. Incline push-up. Incline push-up is a beginner-friendly exercise that targets the chest, shoulders, and triceps. Place your hands on an elevated surface such as a bench, table, or wall slightly wider than shoulder width apart. Keep your body straight from head to heels and engage your core muscles. Slowly lower your chest toward the surface by bending your elbows. Pause briefly when your chest gets close, then push yourself back to the starting position. Maintain controlled movement and steady breathing throughout the exercise. Avoid sagging your hips or lifting them too high. This exercise helps improve upper body strength and chest development. Exercise number six. Landmine kneeling squeeze press. Landmine kneeling squeeze press is a great exercise for building the upper chest, shoulders, and triceps. Kneel on the floor while holding the end of a landmine barbell with both hands close to your chest. Keep your core tight and maintain an upright posture. Press the bar forward and slightly upward while squeezing your hands together to increase chest activation. Fully extend your arms without locking the elbows. Then slowly bring the bar back to the starting position in a controlled motion. Keep steady breathing [bell] and avoid using momentum. This exercise improves chest strength, stability, and upper body muscle control. Exercise number seven. Decline lever lying chest press plate loaded. Decline lever lying chest press plate loaded is an effective exercise for targeting the lower chest, shoulders, and triceps. Lie on the decline chest press machine with your back firmly against the pad and feet secured properly. Grip the handles at chest level and keep your elbows slightly below your shoulders. Push the handles forward until your arms are almost fully extended without locking the elbows. Slowly return the handles to the starting position in a controlled motion while keeping tension on the chest muscles. Maintain steady breathing and avoid jerking movements. This exercise helps improve lower chest strength, muscle size, and pressing power. Exercise number eight. Cable middle fly. Cable middle fly is an excellent exercise for targeting the middle chest muscles and improving chest definition. Stand between two cable machines and hold the handles with your arms extended slightly outward at shoulder level. Keep a slight bend in your elbows and maintain a stable stance. Slowly bring both handles together in front of your chest while squeezing your chest muscles. Pause briefly at the center, then slowly return to the starting position in a controlled motion. Avoid using momentum or excessive weight. Keep your chest lifted and core engaged throughout the exercise. This workout mainly trains the chest, shoulders, and supporting arm muscles.

[music] >> Exercise number one. One arm dumbbell standing concentration curl. Stand upright holding a dumbbell in one hand. Bend slightly forward and place your free hand on your thigh or a stable surface for support. Let the working arm hang straight down. Keeping your elbow close to your body, curl the dumbbell upward toward your shoulder focusing on squeezing the biceps. Avoid swinging your body and keep the movement controlled. Pause briefly at the top, then slowly lower the dumbbell back to the starting position. Maintain proper form throughout. This exercise targets the biceps helping improve peak strength and muscle definition. Exercise number two. Alternating dumbbell curl with 90° hold. Stand upright holding a dumbbell in each hand with palms facing forward. Curl one dumbbell up while keeping the other arm bent at a 90° angle holding it steady. Squeeze the biceps at the top, then slowly lower the moving arm. Now switch sides. Curl the other dumbbell while the first arm holds at 90°. Keep your elbows close to your body and avoid swinging. Maintain control throughout the movement. This exercise increases time under tension targeting the biceps effectively to build strength, size, and better muscle endurance. Exercise number three. Weighted plate standing biceps curl. Stand upright holding a weight plate with both hands gripping it firmly at the sides. Keep your feet shoulder width apart, back straight, and core engaged. Let your arms hang fully extended in front of your thighs. Slowly curl the plate upward toward your chest by bending your elbows, keeping them close to your body. Squeeze your biceps at the top and hold briefly. Then lower the plate back down in a controlled manner. Avoid using momentum or [bell] swinging. This exercise targets the biceps, helping to improve strength, size, and overall arm definition. Exercise number four. Dumbbell standing hammer or drag curl. Stand upright holding a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral grip, palms facing your body. Keep your chest up and shoulders back. Instead of curling outward, drag the dumbbells up along your torso by pulling your elbows backward. Keep the weights close to your body as you lift them toward your upper abs or lower chest. Squeeze your biceps at the top, then slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position. Avoid swinging or using momentum. >> [bell] >> This exercise targets the biceps, especially the long head, helping build thickness and overall arm size. Exercise number five. Dumbbell lying supine curl. Lie flat on a bench holding a dumbbell in each hand, arms fully extended toward the floor with palms facing forward. Keep your back and head supported on the bench. Slowly curl the dumbbells upward by bending your elbows, keeping your upper arms still. Bring the weights toward your shoulders and squeeze your biceps at the top. Pause briefly, then lower the dumbbells back to the starting position in a controlled manner. Avoid swinging or lifting your shoulders. This exercise targets the biceps, helping improve muscle stretch, strength, and overall arm development. Exercise number six. Easy barbell seated curls. Sit on a bench holding an easy bar with an underhand grip and shoulder width apart. Keep your back straight, chest up, and feet firmly on the floor. Let your arms fully extend down in front of you. Curl the bar upward toward your chest by bending your elbows, keeping them close to your body. Squeeze your biceps at the top and pause briefly. Then slowly lower the bar back to the starting position with control. Avoid swinging or using momentum. >> [bell] >> This exercise targets the biceps, helping to build strength, size, and better muscle definition. Exercise number seven. Cable unilateral bicep curl. Stand beside a cable machine with the handle set at a low position. Hold the handle with one hand using an underhand grip, arm fully extended. Keep your back straight, core engaged, and elbow close to your body. Curl the handle upward toward your shoulder by bending your elbow, keeping the upper arm still. Squeeze your biceps at the top and pause briefly. Then slowly lower the handle back to the starting position with control. Avoid using momentum or swinging. This exercise targets the biceps, improving strength, balance, and muscle definition. Exercise number eight. Lever drag bicep curl. Stand upright at a lever machine station and grip the handles with an underhand grip. Keep your chest up, back straight, and elbows close to your body. Start with your arms fully extended. Instead of lifting outward, drag the handles upward along your torso by pulling your elbows slightly backward. Keep the movement controlled and the weights close to your body. Squeeze your biceps at the top, then slowly lower the handles back to the starting position. Avoid using momentum. This exercise targets the biceps, especially the long head, helping build thickness and better arm shape.

[music] >> Exercise number one. Standing dumbbell shoulder press. Stand upright with feet shoulder width apart, holding a dumbbell in each hand at shoulder height, palms facing forward. Keep your core tight and back straight. Press the dumbbells upward until your arms are fully extended overhead without locking your elbows. Pause briefly at the top, then slowly lower the weights back to the starting position. Maintain control throughout the movement and avoid arching your lower back. This exercise primarily targets the shoulders, especially the deltoids, while also engaging the triceps and upper chest for stability and strength. Exercise number two. Easy bar upright rows. Stand upright holding an easy bar with an overhead grip, hands slightly closer than shoulder width. Let the bar rest in front of your thighs. Keep your back straight and core engaged. Pull the bar upward along your body by lifting your elbows, keeping them higher than your wrists. Raise the bar until it reaches chest or upper chest level. Pause briefly at the top, then slowly lower it back to the starting position. Avoid using momentum >> [bell] >> and keep the movement controlled. This exercise mainly targets the shoulders, deltoids, and trapezius muscles. Exercise number three. Standing cable lateral raises. Stand beside a cable machine with the handle set at the lowest position. Hold the handle with one hand, keeping your arm slightly bent and your core tight. Stand upright with feet shoulder width apart. Raise your arm out to the side until it reaches shoulder height, keeping your movement controlled. Pause briefly at the top, then slowly lower the handle back to the starting position. Avoid swinging your body or using momentum. Repeat for desired reps, then switch sides. This exercise mainly targets the lateral deltoids, helping to build wider shoulders. Exercise number four. Seated dumbbell lateral raises. Sit on a bench with a dumbbell in each hand, arms resting at your sides and palms facing inward. Keep your back straight and core engaged. Slightly bend your elbows and raise the dumbbells out to the sides until your arms reach shoulder height. Pause briefly at the top, feeling the contraction in your shoulders. Slowly lower the weights back to the starting position with control. Avoid swinging or using momentum. Keep movements smooth and steady. This exercise mainly targets the lateral deltoids, helping to build shoulder width and improve overall shoulder strength. Exercise number five. Standing dumbbell shrugs. Stand upright with a dumbbell in each hand, arms fully extended at your sides and palms facing your body. Keep your feet shoulder width apart and your core engaged. Slowly lift your shoulders straight upward toward your ears without bending your elbows. Squeeze your trapezius muscles at the top and hold for a second. Then slowly lower your shoulders back to the starting position in a controlled manner. Avoid rolling your shoulders or using momentum. Keep the movement strict and vertical. This exercise mainly targets the trapezius muscles, helping to build upper back and neck strength. Exercise number six. Seated Smith machine shoulder press. Sit on a bench under a Smith machine with the bar set at shoulder height. Grip the bar slightly wider than shoulder width. Palms facing forward. Keep your back straight and core engaged. Press the bar upward until your arms are fully extended without locking your elbows. Pause briefly at the top. Then slowly lower the bar back to shoulder level in a controlled manner. Keep your head neutral and avoid arching your lower back. Maintain steady movement throughout. This exercise mainly targets the deltoids, especially the anterior and lateral heads, along with the triceps. >> Mhm.
